skill.name: pymoo
skill.description: Guide and examples for solving single-objective, multi-objective, constrained, and many-objective optimization problems with pymoo.
skill.summary: Build pymoo optimization workflows with algorithms, constraints, benchmarks, visualization, and decision-making guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but manual review found they are Markdown Python examples and optimization algorithm names such as GA and NSGA, not shell execution or cryptographic code. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network behavior was found. Residual low risk comes from fixed example commands that run local scripts and a documentation URL.

low_findings.title: Markdown Python Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many fenced code examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Reviewed locations show normal pymoo Python imports, algorithm configuration, and plotting examples, with no subprocess, eval, or shell API use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is fenced Python documentation for pymoo usage. The executable examples contain ordinary imports and optimization calls, not command execution primitives.

low_findings.title: Optimization Algorithm Names Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: High-severity weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by terms like GA, NSGA-II, and NSGA-III. These are evolutionary optimization algorithms, not cryptographic primitives or password hashing logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines discuss optimization algorithms and visualization labels. No cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, key handling, or authentication context is present.

low_findings.title: Fixed Local Commands and Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The skill includes fixed commands to run local example scripts and install pymoo, plus a hardcoded documentation URL. These are expected for a Python library guide and do not include user-controlled command construction or network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are static and limited to local example execution and package installation. The only URL found is the public pymoo documentation link, so residual risk is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Optimization work is hard when algorithms, constraints, and trade-off analysis are scattered across many examples. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured pymoo workflows, references, and runnable examples.
actual_capabilities: Guides selection of pymoo algorithms for single-objective, multi-objective, many-objective, constrained, and dynamic problems.
Provides example workflows for NSGA-II, NSGA-III, genetic algorithms, custom problems, constraints, and decision making.
Explains benchmark problem families including ZDT, DTLZ, WFG, MW, DAS-CMOP, and engineering design problems.
Documents genetic sampling, selection, crossover, mutation, and repair operators with configuration guidance.
Covers visualization choices such as scatter plots, parallel coordinates, heatmaps, petal diagrams, radar charts, and animations.
Includes runnable local Python examples for common pymoo optimization workflows.
limitations: It does not install pymoo automatically; users must manage their own Python environment.
It provides examples and guidance, not guaranteed optimal parameters for every real-world problem.
It focuses on pymoo 0.6.x patterns, so newer API changes may require verification.
Visualization examples may require a graphical backend or notebook environment.

use_cases.title: Compare Engineering Design Trade-offs
use_cases.description: Model competing objectives such as cost, weight, and performance, then inspect Pareto-optimal solution sets.

use_cases.title: Prototype Benchmark Studies
use_cases.description: Use standard ZDT, DTLZ, WFG, and constrained problems to compare algorithm behavior under controlled conditions.

use_cases.title: Select a Preferred Pareto Solution
use_cases.description: Normalize objective values, apply preference weights, and visualize candidate solutions for decision review.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Algorithm
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection for a new optimization task
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an optimization problem with [number] objectives, [variable types], and [constraint details]. Recommend a pymoo algorithm and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Custom Problem
prompt_templates.scenario: Define objectives and constraints in pymoo
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a pymoo ElementwiseProblem for these objectives and constraints: [describe formulas, bounds, and feasibility rules].

prompt_templates.title: Tune Operators and Termination
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve an existing evolutionary optimization run
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pymoo setup for [algorithm]. Suggest sampling, crossover, mutation, population size, and termination settings for [problem characteristics].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Pareto Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a Pareto front with objectives [objectives] and preferences [weights or priorities], recommend normalization, MCDM method, and visualizations.

output_examples.input: User asks which algorithm to use for three conflicting engineering objectives with constraints.
output: Recommendation: start with NSGA-II because the problem has two or three objectives and needs diverse Pareto coverage.
Use feasibility-first constraint handling first, then try penalty or constraint-as-objective methods if feasible solutions are rare.
Review scatter plots and petal diagrams to compare trade-offs before choosing a final design.

output_examples.input: User asks how to evaluate a many-objective optimization result.
output: Use NSGA-III with reference directions for four or more objectives.
Inspect the front with parallel coordinate plots and normalize axes when objective scales differ.
Apply preference weights only after stakeholders confirm that the objective scales and priorities are valid.

output_examples.input: User asks why a constrained pymoo run returns mostly infeasible solutions.
output: Check that inequality constraints are formulated as values less than or equal to zero.
Compare feasibility-first handling with penalty wrapping, repair, or constraint-as-objective approaches.
Increase population size or generations if the feasible region is small or disconnected.

best_practices: Normalize objectives before comparing solutions or applying multi-criteria decision methods.
Set random seeds and save history when comparing algorithm performance.
Start with standard pymoo benchmarks before moving to custom real-world problem definitions.
anti_patterns: Do not use NSGA-III without defining appropriate reference directions.
Do not compare objectives with different scales before normalization.
Do not assume a single Pareto solution is best without stating decision preferences.

faq.question: What is pymoo used for?
faq.answer: pymoo is used for single-objective, multi-objective, many-objective, constrained, and evolutionary optimization in Python.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete optimization code?
faq.answer: It provides workflows and examples that help Claude, Codex, or Claude Code produce pymoo implementations for your problem.

faq.question: Which algorithm should I start with?
faq.answer: Use GA or DE for single-objective problems, NSGA-II for two or three objectives, and NSGA-III for four or more objectives.

faq.question: Can it help with constraints?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers feasibility-first handling, penalties, constraint-as-objective methods, repair operators, SRES, and ISRES.

faq.question: Can it help choose one Pareto solution?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents normalization, pseudo-weights, compromise programming, knee points, and visualization methods for decision making.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No runtime network access is required by the skill content. It includes a public documentation link for reference.
